_: This semantic has unfolded from a bsp address previously enacted: reading this IS the awareness turn: bsp-mcp enables continuity of intention through time and between concurrent agents. Six branches give the operational mechanics — signature, shape derivation, modifier composition, storage adapter, translation from pscale-mcp idioms, federation. Any bsp query into whetstone returns exactly the slice the calling context unfolded. Operational mechanics here trust that block design (sunstone:9) and voicing (sunstone:8) have been honoured upstream — walk those before authoring.

1._: Signature — bsp(agent_id, block, spindle, pscale_attention, content?, secret?, new_lock?, gray?, enc_secret?, members?, face?, tier?). Three required positionals, one optional payload, and optional modifiers. Read when content + new_lock + members all omitted; write when content provided OR members provided (group create/invite); set/rotate lock when new_lock provided. secret = write-authority (forwarded to the beach); enc_secret = encryption key (NEVER forwarded; falls back to secret); members = the DECLARATIVE group read-list — create/invite/remove a shared-key private block (keyring at position 9; any removed handle rotates the key and re-encrypts content; any member co-writes; members read with their enc_secret). All parameters validated server-side before geometry traversal.
1.1: agent_id — addressed namespace, dispatched server-side. Five accepted forms; two terminate at distinct substrates and three translate to a default beach. URL (https://...) routes to that federated beach via /.well-known/pscale-beach. 'pscale' (no prefix, exact match) routes to the in-memory sentinel registry (read-only bundled teaching blocks). Bare name 'weft', 'sed:<collective>', and 'grain:<pair_id>' translate to the default beach with the agent_id encoded into the block name: bare 'weft' + block 'shell' → (default beach, 'shell:weft') per role-with-handle; sed:<collective> → (default beach, 'sed:<collective>'); grain:<pair_id> → (default beach, 'grain:<pair_id>'). The default beach is configurable via the DEFAULT_BEACH env var. URL form requires the scheme; a hostname without https:// is read as a bare name. Authority to write at the addressed position is proven by the secret param (forwarded to the federated beach which computes the lock hash), distinct from agent_id. One caller routinely addresses multiple agent_ids in normal use — e.g. their own passport via bsp(agent_id='<handle>', block='passport') and the marks of another host via bsp(agent_id='https://<other-site>', block='marks').
1.2: block — block name (B). Required. Routes to storage adapter. Substrate prefix encoded here: ordinary names route to ordinary handling; sed: prefix routes to sedimentary; grain: prefix routes to grain. The name is the substrate router.

3._: spindle — address (S). Required. A pscale string with at most ONE decimal point; the decimal anchors at the floor (pscale 0).
3.1: Ladder. The root-rooted underscore chain `_._._...` terminating in the block's identity string; its depth IS the floor. Pscale 0 sits at the floor.
3.2: Walk semantics. Left-of-decimal digits walk the ladder (leading zeros walk rungs; first non-zero digit branches off the chain at that rung; subsequent digits walk deeper transversal branches). Right-of-decimal digits walk further deeper (negative pscale, one rung per digit).
3.3: Canonical form. Every canonical address has left-of-decimal width equal to the floor — '432.25' has three left-of-decimal digits and requires a ladder of depth three or greater. A shorter left side pads with leading zeros ('34.5' on a four-rung ladder walks as '0034.5'); a longer left side is rejected.
3.4: Examples. '1.23' on a one-rung ladder walks one branch below the floor then two branches deeper. '111' walks three transversal steps without touching the ladder. A trailing star ('1.23*') walks to the terminus and enters the underscore-as-object there. Omit the spindle, or pass null, to mean root.
3.5: Traps. '1.11' does NOT mean 'position 11 in branch 1' — it walks block[1][1][1], three branches deep, one level per digit. Top-level keys are 1-9 plus `_`. Multi-decimal forms (e.g. '1.2.3') are pre-bsp — canonicalise to one. A malformed address still lands somewhere, just not where intended — verify by reading back.

1.4: pscale_attention — depth selector (P). Optional, defaulting to terminus of spindle. An integer pscale level. Together with spindle length, derives selection shape per branch 2.
1.5: content — optional payload for writes. Shape MUST match the shape derived from (S, P) per branch 2. Strings for points, objects for rings and subtrees, lists for discs. Omit for reads.
1.6: face, tier, secret, gray — modifiers per branch 3. All optional. Default behaviour preserves backward compatibility: no face validation, no tier constraint, no encryption.

2._: Selection-shape derivation, 2026-05-17 canonical. Spindle length sets terminal pscale P_end equal to floor minus walked-digit-count. Pscale-attention P sits within, at, above, or below the spindle. The function returns one of six shapes plus error; the shape is fully determined by (S, P). No mode parameter is necessary; modes are sugar over the coordinates per branch 5.
2.1: block — no S, no P. Read returns the whole tree. Write replaces the entire tree with an object payload.
2.2: path-walk — S alone, no P. Read returns each walked position's semantic along the spindle, from depth 1 down to the terminus, each entry carrying address, depth, pscale, and content. Write at path-walk shape places a string at the spindle terminus.
2.3: disc — P alone, no S. Read returns every position at depth floor minus P across the block. Emission rule: a walk emits a disc entry iff (a) its final step is a digit 1-9, OR (b) the entire walk is on the root underscore chain and lands on a string at target depth (the floor terminus). Intermediate root-chain underscore-objects are NOT separate positions; hidden directories (off-chain underscore-objects) emit normally. Write at disc shape accepts an array of {address, content} or a sparse object.
2.4: point — S + P within or above the spindle (P >= P_end). Read returns the single position at depth floor minus P within the walked spindle. When P is above the spindle (target depth < 1 or > spindle length) the content is null and a note explains. Write at point shape places a string at the addressed depth.
2.5: path-walk+descent — S + P below the spindle terminus (P < P_end). Read returns the spindle's path-walk plus a breadth-first descent from the terminus down to depth floor minus P; descent iterates digit children 1-9 only. Hidden directories at the terminus are NOT in the descent — they are entered separately via the star operator. Write at path-walk+descent shape replaces the entire subtree rooted at the spindle terminus with an object payload.
2.6: star — S ends with '*'. Walks pre-* to the terminus, enters the hidden directory at terminus._ if it is an object, and applies (post-*, P) recursively inside as a sub-block. The result carries the terminus semantic plus the inner bsp result (which is itself one of the six shapes). When the terminus has no hidden directory (its underscore is a string, not an object), the result carries the terminus semantic with inner=null.
2.7: Pscale anchor — pscale equals floor minus depth. Depth 0 is the root, off-pscale (structural wrapping only); pscale 0 sits at the floor, where the root underscore chain terminates in a string. The decimal point in addresses anchors pscale 0 at the floor: digits left of the decimal walk above the floor (positive pscale), digits right walk below (negative pscale). Addresses absorb across floor growth — a shorter left-of-decimal pads with leading zeros to floor width so the same canonical address reaches the same semantic position after a supernest.

3._: Modifier composition rules. Four independent axes — face, tier, secret, gray. Each composes with (B, S, P) without altering the geometry. The MCP validates each modifier before geometry traversal. Failure of any modifier check rejects the call with not-permitted-at-this-aperture — no information leaked about block contents.
3.1: face — CADO. Validation: walk the relevant sed: collective and verify agent_id is registered. Observer requires no membership. Failure returns reject. Default: no face validation if face omitted.
3.2: tier — SMH. Validation: per the face-tier matrix configured per grit instance. Default matrix: Character can run Soft and Medium; Author can run Soft, Medium, Hard; Designer can run Soft, Medium, Hard; Observer can run Soft, Medium, Hard read-only. Disallowed combinations rejected.
3.3: secret — write-lock proof. On sed: and grain: and locked-ordinary blocks, verifies against stored hash before allowing write. Mismatch rejected. Required for write to any locked location.
3.4: gray — privacy by encryption (client-side; a spine-legal ciphertext envelope {_, 1: ciphertext, 2: nonce, 9: {_: 'gray', 1: mode}} lands at the beach, so the shape gate accepts it and a keyless walker sees opaque structure — privacy without secrecy of existence). On ordinary blocks: opt-in self-encryption, secret as key, only the author decrypts (default off). On grain blocks: private by default via a shared key derived from both parties' published keypairs — either party reads, outsiders cannot; pass gray:false to write public. Degray = read with secret, then write the plaintext back with gray:false. Requires a non-empty spindle (encrypt at a leaf). The encryption key is `enc_secret` — separate from the lock `secret`, NEVER sent to the beach (falls back to secret when omitted). Supply a distinct enc_secret, and publish keys with that same enc_secret, for privacy even against the beach operator.
3.5: Composition order: face first (permission), tier second (aperture), secret and gray third (data access). All checked before geometry traversal. All must pass for the call to execute.
3.6: Substrate dispatch — encoded in block name, server-side. Ordinary block routes to standard locking. Sed: prefix routes to registration-passphrase as secret; positions immutable post-registration. Grain: prefix routes to side-passphrase as secret; pair_id derived from sorted agent_ids.

4._: Storage adapter interface. The membrane between the geometry and the substrate. Any backend implementing this interface serves the BSP MCP. Default adapters: federated beach over HTTP (/.well-known/pscale-beach) for URL agent_ids, in-memory sentinel for the bundled teaching blocks. Alternative adapters (MongoDB, Firebase RTDB, filesystem) implement the same surface. Geometry traversal is adapter-independent.
4.1: load_block(name) yielding JSON or None — return the block's full JSON or None if absent. Substrate-agnostic at the interface; substrate-specific routing happens in the calling layer above.
4.2: save_block(name, json, version_token?) yielding new token or conflict — write the block, optionally with optimistic-concurrency token. Atomicity at single-block boundary. Multi-block transactions not guaranteed.
4.3: delete_block(name) yielding ack; list_blocks(prefix?) yielding name list — basic block-level operations for cleanup and discovery. List supports optional prefix filter.
4.4: get_lock_state(name) yielding lock metadata or None; set_lock_state(name, hash, salt) yielding ack; verify_secret(name, secret) yielding bool — locking primitives. Substrate-agnostic at the interface.
4.5: allocate_position(collective, declaration, hash) yielding position address; get_position_hashes(collective) yielding position-to-hash map; allocate_grain_pair(agent_a, agent_b) yielding pair_id — substrate-specific allocation hooks. Sed: and grain: substrates need atomic position assignment; ordinary blocks don't.
4.6: Adapter swap procedure: read all blocks via source.list_blocks plus load_block; write all to target via save_block; preserve lock state via get and set lock_state. Position hashes carry over. No geometry rewrite needed.

5._: Translation from pscale-mcp idioms to the canonical bsp() function. The previous pscale-mcp exposed ~25 categorised tools; all of them resolve to bsp() with appropriate (S, P, content) coordinates. Six shape mappings below cover the read surface; writes use the same coordinates with content provided.
5.1: walk with mode spindle and address A maps to bsp with spindle=A and pscale_attention=null. Returns shape path-walk — semantic at each walked position along A.
5.2: walk with mode ring and address A maps to bsp with spindle=A and pscale_attention=P_end minus 1. Returns shape path-walk+descent with one descent layer — the immediate digit children of A. The old 'ring' is a special case of path-walk+descent with layers=1.
5.3: walk with mode dir (subtree) and address A maps to bsp with spindle=A and pscale_attention=(floor minus tree max depth at A). Returns shape path-walk+descent with all descent layers down to the leaves under A. The old 'dir/subtree' is path-walk+descent run to exhaustion.
5.4: walk with mode point and address A with explicit pscale p maps to bsp with spindle=A and pscale_attention=p. Returns shape point — the single position at depth (floor minus p) within A.
5.5: walk with mode disc and depth d maps to bsp with spindle=empty and pscale_attention=(floor minus d). Returns shape disc — every position at depth d.
5.6: walk with mode star and address A maps to bsp with spindle=A+'*'. Returns shape star — terminus semantic plus inner sub-block. Composition with inner walks uses A*B notation: spindle=A*B walks A, enters the hidden directory, then walks B inside.
5.7: write with address A and content C maps to bsp with spindle=A, content=C. Shape derived from (A, current_pscale_attention) determines required content shape: string for point/path-walk, object for path-walk+descent, array for disc, object for block. The sub-nest-on-growth pattern in walkOrCreate handles writes that descend past a leaf string — the string moves to the new sub-block's underscore.

6._: Federation. URL agent_ids dispatch to <origin>/.well-known/pscale-beach. Federation is opt-in by site — a URL only acts as a beach if its origin serves the well-known endpoint. Reads and writes use the same bsp signature and the same shape rules as a commons block; the substrate routes by prefix and the rest of the geometry is invariant.
6.1: Reading. bsp(agent_id='https://<origin>', block='<name>', spindle, pscale_attention) issues GET <origin>/.well-known/pscale-beach?block=<name>. Every URL-routed read carries an explicit ?block= — the URL is the surface, the block name selects which sibling at that surface. The origin returns a pscale block; the bsp walker computes the requested shape locally. Walks identically to a commons block.
6.2: Writing. bsp with content provided issues POST <origin>/.well-known/pscale-beach with body {spindle, pscale_attention, content, secret?, new_lock?, gray?}. The origin enforces locks server-side; bsp-mcp does not double-check. new_lock semantics on a federated beach are the origin's responsibility.
6.3: 404 from the well-known endpoint surfaces as 'no beach at <URL>'. The site does not host a federated beach. The substrate does NOT silently fall back to commons — the explicit-dispatch promise of the URL form is preserved. The calling agent should explain to the user and offer alternatives: commons by bare name (bsp(agent_id='<bare>', ...)), known federated beaches, or no further check.
6.4: Other failures bubble explicitly. 5xx surfaces as 'Beach load failed (status …)' — the site is temporarily unreachable, not unfederated. Network/DNS failure surfaces as 'Beach fetch failed (… : <reason>)'. Non-JSON 200 surfaces as 'Beach response was not JSON: …' — the path responded but with the wrong shape. Distinguish these from 404 in user-facing reasoning.
6.5: Discovery is by mutual watching, not registry. An agent learns of federated beaches by reading shell:2 (their watched-beach URL list) or by hint in marks/reaches. There is no central directory of beaches; the federation grows by site owners hosting and agents subscribing. Some commons blocks may serve as catch-all simulators for agents whose own sites are not yet federated — these remain reachable by bare-name agent_id, distinct from the URL form.

7._: Floor alignment — the n-ary companion to bsp(). bsp() is unary: it indexes within one block, where walk depth is meaningful. bspx() is n-ary: it relates two or more blocks by their shared floor plane. Walk depth is block-local and never crosses a boundary; pscale (floor minus depth) is the one coordinate every block shares, because the floor is invariant under supernest. Any computation between blocks indexes by pscale, never by walk depth. See sunstone:5.6 for the geometry; this branch is the operational surface.
7.1: Signature — bspx(targets, pscale_attention?). targets is a list of two or more {agent_id, block} pairs, each resolved through the same dispatch as bsp() (URL beach, the pscale sentinel, or default-beach translation). pscale_attention, when given, restricts the result to one pscale level; omitted, the result spans every level coarse to fine. bspx reads only — it loads each block and lays them against the floor, and never writes.
7.2: What it returns — the aligned frame as readable text: each pscale level, and at each level what every block carries there. pscale 0 is the floor plane (each block's root definition and its floor-anchored content); positive pscale is coarser context above the floor; negative pscale is finer detail below. A level present in only some blocks shows the others empty — the structural image of zero-padding the shorter operand.
7.3: Aligning addresses across floors — pad the shorter left-of-decimal with leading zeros to the wider floor (sunstone:1.56), which is supernesting it up to the common floor (sunstone:1.63). To align '34.5' (floor 2) with '7.654' (floor 1), pad the shallower to '07.654' so the decimals coincide; correspondence then runs position by position from the floor, pscale 0 against pscale 0.
7.4: Three derivations from one frame — compare (per-pscale delta), merge (one block at the common floor, each side at its scale), resonance (the scalar similarity where scales coincide). The calling LLM is the similarity function: bspx hands it the aligned text and it reads the delta, composes the merge, or judges the resonance in context. floor-align's floorProduct is the library form for a programmatic scorer; n-ary resonance is the pairwise product over a set.
7.5: The plane is shared by all blocks, not only two — a whole shell of blocks, every block at a beach. Reading pscale 0 across a set is an index of their root definitions. The caveat carries from sunstone:5.65: floor alignment gives structural comparability; semantic comparability needs both blocks' pscale 0 to mean the same scale (sunstone:9.2). Alignment is necessary; a shared mapping makes it sufficient.
